Images captured under improper exposure conditions suffer from exposure problems.
Improper exposure changes the statistical distribution of image brightness.
Contrast degradation and detail distortion result in detail distortion.
Low-frequency and high-frequency components mix due to contrast degradation and detail distortion.
Image exposure correction becomes extremely challenging due to mixed frequency components.
One solution is to design an end-to-end architecture for learning contrast enhancement and detail restoration.

Contrast enhancement and detail restoration are the main focus of the end-to-end architecture mentioned in the context. The methods mainly focus on contrast enhancement due to the dominance of low-frequency components, which leads to the challenge of efficiently restoring high-frequency details.

The contrast-relevant features are primarily distributed in low-frequency components, while the detail-relevant features are primarily distributed in high-frequency components. However, due to the statistical dominance of low-frequency components, methods mainly focus on contrast enhancement, leading to challenges in efficiently restoring high-frequency details.

Some researchers propose to decompose and restore the input image's lightness and structure components separately to achieve better contrast enhancement and detail restoration. This approach aims to address the challenges of balancing contrast and detail enhancement.

Researchers decompose images into illumination and reflectance components to design specific networks for each component. This approach is based on the Retinex theory and aims to improve contrast enhancement and detail restoration.

Researchers decompose the input image into multi-scale components and adopt a coarse-to-fine strategy to progressively recover lightness and fine-scale structures. This strategy helps in balancing contrast enhancement and detail restoration.

The decomposition operation destroys the relationship between brightness and detail, making it challenging to balance contrast and detail enhancement. This can lead to over-smooth problems or artifacts in the enhanced results.

The paper proposes to decouple contrast enhancement and detail restoration to address the challenges mentioned earlier. By separating these processes, the paper aims to achieve a better balance between contrast enhancement and detail restoration.

Both contrast enhancement and detail restoration are important aspects of image processing. They are related in terms of improving the visual quality of an image by enhancing the contrast and restoring fine details.

The feature response in local regions can be decomposed into low-frequency components and high-frequency components. This decomposition helps in analyzing the different frequency components of an image.
The CA unit and DA unit are introduced to guide contrast and detail modeling. They work in parallel to improve the overall image quality.

DAConv, which incorporates CA, DA, and dynamic coefficients, can substitute the TConv kernel in CNN-based exposure correction networks. This substitution aims to enhance contrast and restore details effectively.

A dynamic coefficient is introduced for each branch to adjust the amplitude of the feature response. This adjustment helps in balancing contrast enhancement and detail restoration in the image processing.

The CA, DA, and dynamic coefficients are aggregated into a single TConv kernel through structural re-parameterization in the inference phase. This aggregation helps in reducing computational costs without compromising the functionality of DAConv.

The linear superposition of CA, DA, and dynamic coefficients helps in reducing computational costs while maintaining the functionality of DAConv. This approach improves network performance without increasing computational overhead.

The convolution process TConv is expressed as a combination of high-frequency and low-frequency responses.

The traditional convolution feature response mixes the low-frequency response and high-frequency response. To separate the high-frequency response, a central-surrounding difference operation is introduced.

With the central-surrounding difference operation, the low-frequency response can be significantly suppressed. A convolution kernel injected with the difference operation is denoted as Detail Aware (DA) kernel.

To obtain the high-frequency response, an intuitive option is to subtract it from the response y(pc). However, direct subtraction leads to a drop in performance due to noise, especially in under-exposure conditions.
To suppress the high-frequency response, the proportion of low-frequency response is increased by injecting the central-surrounding addition operation into the convolution process.
